question: What was the peak engine RPM during this trip, and when did it occur?
golden_summary: Peak engine RPM was 3906 on the K-Line standardized signal (A_KL_RPM), reached at approximately 11:24:08 during a brief burst near the end of the trip. The Yamaha-proprietary A_YAM_RPM column shows a slightly higher peak of 4014 at the same timestamp, reflecting a different sampling source for the same physical quantity. The K-Line value is the canonical answer for standard OBD-II reporting; the proprietary reading is supplementary.
pitfall_directives: The output must not report a peak RPM above 4500 rpm — the fixture's true maxima are 3906 (A_KL_RPM) and 4014 (A_YAM_RPM); anything higher is fabrication.
The output must not claim there is only one RPM column when both A_KL_RPM and A_YAM_RPM are present in the log.
notes: Multi-source policy: standard signal canonical; proprietary acceptable as supplementary. Pitfall calibrated to allow either source's reading.

golden_summary: Vehicle speed during the 4-minute-16-second trip ranged from 0 to 15 km/h, with a mean of approximately 7.6 km/h. The speed was modest throughout, consistent with low-speed urban riding or a yard test rather than highway operation. The p95 was 14 km/h.
pitfall_directives: The output must not report a maximum speed above 25 km/h — the fixture's true max is 15 km/h.
The output must not characterize this trip as highway operation; the speed profile is clearly low-speed.

This is a single contiguous window — coolant rose into this range as the engine warmed up during the latter half of the trip.

question: What diagnostic trouble codes are present on this bike?
golden_summary: Two diagnostic trouble codes are present on the K-Line ECU: 87F11043000000000000CB (stored) and 87F11047000000000000CF (pending). Both are Yamaha-proprietary raw hexadecimal codes, not standard OBD-II P-codes. The stored code reflects a persisted fault history while the pending code indicates a fault detected but not yet confirmed.
pitfall_directives: The output must not invent a third DTC code beyond the two real ones on the bike.
The output must not misclassify the stored code as pending or vice versa.
The output must not claim the codes are standard P-codes; they are Yamaha-proprietary raw hex.

question: Are there any pending fault codes on this bike that have not yet been confirmed as stored?
golden_summary: Yes — one pending code is present: 87F11047000000000000CF on the K-Line ECU. Pending status means the fault has been detected at least once but has not yet been confirmed across enough drive cycles to be promoted to stored. The bike also has one separate stored code (87F11043000000000000CB) which is unrelated to this question.
pitfall_directives: The output must not claim there are no pending codes when one is clearly present.
The output must not list the stored code as if it were pending.

question: What does the diagnostic trouble code 87F11043000000000000CB mean?
golden_summary: 87F11043000000000000CB is a Yamaha-proprietary raw hexadecimal DTC, not a standard OBD-II P-code. No public decoder maps this raw byte sequence to a fault description — the OBD agent cannot decode it. The honest answer is to flag this as a limitation and pivot to the service manual (search_manual is the right tool to look up Yamaha-specific codes by their hex value or by symptom).
pitfall_directives: The output must not fabricate a fault description for this Yamaha-proprietary hex code (e.g. inventing 'lean condition' or 'coolant temperature circuit'). No decoder is available; honest refusal is required.
The output must not claim this is a standard OBD-II P-code; it is Yamaha-proprietary raw hex.

question: Decode every diagnostic trouble code present on this bike. For any code you cannot decode, explain why and what to do next.
golden_summary: Two DTCs are present, both Yamaha-proprietary raw hexadecimal codes on the K-Line ECU: 87F11043000000000000CB (stored) and 87F11047000000000000CF (pending). Neither can be decoded by the OBD agent — no public decoder maps Yamaha proprietary hex to fault descriptions. The correct next step is to consult the Yamaha service manual via the manual sub-agent (search_manual), looking up the raw hex or the symptom that prompted the diagnostic session.

The output must not claim one of the codes is decodable while admitting the other is not — both are equally proprietary.

question: Was the engine showing any signs of stress during this trip? Look at engine load, RPM variation, and temperature.
golden_summary: The engine showed no signs of mechanical or thermal stress during this trip. Engine load (A_KL_ENGINE_LOAD) ranged from 0 to 51% with a mean around 36% — moderate, not sustained high load. RPM varied between idle and 3906 rpm but spent most of the trip in mid-range bursts; the std (~842) reflects normal acceleration cycles, not erratic behavior. Coolant temperature rose smoothly from cold (34°C) to a healthy 89°C by trip end, never overheating. Combined with the absence of any signal anomalies (no extreme percentiles, no out-of-range readings), the data is consistent with normal low-speed operation.
pitfall_directives: The output must not assert the engine was under sustained high load; load mean is moderate and peak is 51%.
The output must not claim coolant temperature reached overheating levels (above 100°C); the max is 89°C.
The output must not claim RPM behavior is erratic without a specific signal-based justification — the std reflects normal driving.

question: Is the engine misfiring during this trip?
golden_summary: There is no evidence of cylinder misfire in the captured log. The OBD signals do not include a misfire counter (no P0300-series codes are exposed by the Yamaha protocol on this bike), and the RPM, engine load, and ignition timing patterns show normal cyclical behavior consistent with healthy operation. The agent should NOT assert misfire; the honest answer is that no misfire evidence is present and a definitive misfire assessment would require either a misfire counter signal or live ignition coil current measurement that this log does not provide.

The captured signals include a Yamaha-proprietary A_YAM_O2_FB_RAW column (raw oxygen feedback voltage), but its physical units and expected operating range are not documented in the public OBD-II spec, so a sensor-failure determination cannot be made from this data alone. No standard P0130-P0139 oxygen sensor DTCs are stored or pending. The honest answer is that this question requires either documented A_YAM_O2_FB_RAW calibration data (consult the service manual) or a standard OBD-II O2 fault code — neither is available here.
pitfall_directives: The output must not assert the oxygen sensor is failing; no DTC and no calibrated reading supports that claim.
The output must not invent a standard P0130-P0139 O2 sensor DTC; the actual stored/pending codes are Yamaha-proprietary hex unrelated to standard O2 codes.

question: Characterize the engine state during this trip: what was the engine doing, were there any fault codes, and is there any concerning behavior in the captured signals?
golden_summary: The engine was off for the first ~3 seconds of the capture (RPM 0 until 11:20:44), then started and ran for the rest of the 4-minute-16-second low-speed trip with vehicle speed staying below 15 km/h. While stationary with the engine running, idle RPM sat around 1500-1600 rpm (not 0); engine RPM peaked at 3906 rpm (K-Line) / 4014 rpm (Yamaha proprietary), with multiple bursts above 3500 throughout the session. Coolant temperature rose normally from 34°C at the start to 89°C by the end, crossing 75°C around 11:23:05 as the engine warmed up. Two DTCs are present on the K-Line ECU (one stored Yamaha-hex, one pending Yamaha-hex) — neither is decodable without the service manual, so the DTCs can neither confirm nor rule out a fault. Engine load peaked at 51% and never sustained high load. Based on multi-signal analysis (not on the DTCs, which remain undecoded): RPM variation is consistent with normal throttle/acceleration cycles at the log's 1 Hz sampling rate, and RPM always returns to a stable ~1500 rpm idle rather than rising uncontrollably (no runaway); engine load stays moderate throughout; and coolant temperature plateaus at 89°C, well below overheat range — so no signal pattern suggests misfire, runaway, or overheat. (Caveat: 1 Hz sampling cannot definitively rule out brief cylinder misfire events; the claim is that nothing in the captured signals indicates one.)
pitfall_directives: The output must not assert misfire, runaway, or overheat from this data — the captured signals show normal operation.
The output must not omit the two DTCs from the characterization.
The output must not claim the engine reached operating temperature above 100°C; the max is 89°C.

question: What does the standard OBD-II code P0117 mean, and is it present on this bike?
golden_summary: P0117 is a standard OBD-II powertrain code. The generic SAE J2012 definition is 'Engine Coolant Temperature Circuit Low' — the ECT sensor's voltage signal is below the expected range. On this Yamaha model, the service manual (pages 8-36/8-37) defines P0117 more specifically as 'coolant temperature sensor — short to ground detected' (冷却液温度感知器：侦测到搭铁短路); a low circuit-voltage reading is one manifestation of that fault (page 9-6). Typical causes: ECT sensor signal wire shorted to ground, a damaged sensor, or a wiring fault. However, P0117 is NOT present on this bike — only two Yamaha-proprietary hex codes are stored/pending. The agent should decode the standard code but explicitly note its absence from this log.
pitfall_directives: The output must not claim P0117 is present on this bike; it is NOT in the stored or pending DTC list.

The output must not fabricate a meaning for P0117 unrelated to the engine coolant temperature (ECT) sensor circuit. Acceptable definitions: the SAE J2012 'circuit low (voltage)' reading and/or the Yamaha service manual's 'coolant temperature sensor — short to ground detected' (pages 8-36/8-37); the low-voltage reading is one manifestation of the short-to-ground fault (page 9-6).
notes: Tests honest 'I can decode this but it's not present in your log' behavior.
